:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 814 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 51% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

An inmate serving a single sentence that is not a life sentence and who is not classified as a habitual criminal is eligible for parole after serving one-third of the term. Inmates serving multiple terms are eligible for parole after serving one-third of the aggregate time to which they were sentenced, except that inmates serving two or more concurrent terms must serve at least one-third of the maximum term they are required to serve. Inmates already serving one sentence, whether in confinement or on parole, who reoffend and are sentenced for a new crime, are not eligible for parole until they have served one-third of the new sentence (and will not receive credit for the time they served under their previous sentence). If an inmate is sentenced for multiple subsequent offenses, parole eligibility is calculated in the same manner as described above for an original sentence involving multiple offenses.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 38% (310 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 297 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 36% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
